title: The EU must confront China’s trade challenge
gist: MERICS analyst Grzegorz Stec argues the EU cannot afford to wait until its October European Council or 2027 to confront China's export-driven trade imbalances, because Beijing is already hardening its defenses (new supply-chain and outbound-investment rules, Huawei's advancing chip production) while a possible far-right win in France's 2027 presidential election could collapse the pro-confrontation coalition.

themes.theme: Political clock: France 2027 and EU elections

insights.claim: France's April 2027 presidential election is the critical near-term deadline for EU China policy, since Emmanuel Macron has been the driving force behind a more assertive EU stance (EV tariffs, economic-security tools) and his departure could remove the bloc's lead advocate for collective action.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: Watch French presidential polling as a leading indicator for EU trade-defense momentum against China; a Macron successor from outside RN would likely preserve the current trajectory.
insights.quote: The most immediate and consequential deadline is France’s April 2027 presidential election.
insights.locator: What happens in the next few months will be critical

insights.claim: Rassemblement National's likely presidential candidate Jordan Bardella has previously backed de-risking and trade-defense measures against China as an MEP, but has also called the EU 'completely obsolete,' signaling that an RN win would likely shift France toward national rather than EU-level responses to China.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: A Bardella presidency would not necessarily soften on China itself, but would likely fragment the EU's collective bargaining power by pulling France toward unilateral action.
insights.locator: With polls pointing to the possibility of a victory

insights.claim: France, Italy, the Netherlands and Lithuania jointly submitted a discussion paper in late May 2026 pushing for stronger EU economic-security tools against China, showing an active pro-confrontation coalition that could dissolve if allied governments change after 2026–2027 elections.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: This four-country bloc is the working coalition to track for EU trade-defense proposals; its cohesion is a proxy for how much of the toolbox actually gets adopted before 2027.
insights.locator: France, Italy, the Netherlands and Lithuania at the end of May

insights.claim: Looming elections in Italy, Spain and Poland are also expected to weaken those countries' appetite for bold EU initiatives on China, narrowing the window in which the EU can agree on a common trade-defense response.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: The EU's effective decision window for hardening China trade policy is likely limited to the second half of 2026, before national elections start eroding the pro-action bloc.
insights.locator: Looming elections in Italy, Spain and Poland

themes.theme: China's export shock and EU populism

insights.claim: The EU is losing an estimated 500 manufacturing jobs per day as Chinese imports pressure Europe's higher-value industrial sectors, according to unnamed estimates cited by the author.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: A concrete, trackable magnitude for the 'China Shock 2.0' narrative; useful as a baseline to monitor whether EU trade-defense measures actually slow the job-loss rate.
insights.quote: the EU is losing around 500 manufacturing jobs a day
insights.locator: Some estimates suggest that the EU is losing

insights.claim: Multiple studies have linked China-driven manufacturing decline and unemployment in Europe to increased support for populist and Eurosceptic parties, creating a feedback loop where trade-policy delay strengthens the very political forces most likely to block future EU-level action.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: Frames China trade exposure as a direct driver of European political fragmentation risk, not just an industrial-policy issue — relevant to sizing populist/Eurosceptic tail risk in EU assets.
insights.locator: Several studies have linked such manufacturing decline

insights.claim: In May 2026, China adopted regulations 834, 835 and 837 to better protect Chinese supply chains, counter extraterritorial measures against its companies, and monitor outbound investments, indicating Beijing is moving faster to insulate itself than the EU is moving to add trade-defense tools.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: China is already operationalizing defensive economic-security law while the EU is still debating; the sequencing advantage favors Beijing in any coming trade confrontation.
insights.quote: it adopted regulations 834, 835 and 837 to better protect Chinese supply chains
insights.locator: In May, it adopted regulations 834, 835 and 837

insights.claim: Huawei's reported expansion of advanced chip production signals China is becoming less vulnerable to Western technology restrictions, which could erode the leverage Europe derives from Dutch equipment maker ASML's dominance in chipmaking tools.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: A direct read-through for ASML and the broader chip-equipment export-control regime: if Huawei's domestic chip progress continues, the EU/Dutch leverage over China's semiconductor supply is shrinking faster than policy debates assume.
insights.quote: potentially eroding the leverage Europe derives from Dutch company ASML’s dominance in chip making equipment
insights.locator: Huawei’s reported expansion of advanced chip production

insights.claim: China's rare-earth export-licensing regime requires European companies seeking licenses to disclose details about their operations, customers and supply chains, effectively functioning as an intelligence-gathering mechanism on European industry's vulnerabilities.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: European firms dependent on Chinese rare-earth licenses are effectively handing Beijing supply-chain mapping data — a structural asymmetry worth flagging for any company reliant on these licenses.
insights.quote: requires European companies to provide details about their operations, customers and supply chains

insights.claim: The EU's draft Industrial Accelerator Act contains 'Buy European' provisions, a proposed revision of the Cybersecurity Act could restrict market access for Chinese high-risk technology suppliers, and a possible 'diversification instrument' would require companies to reduce excessive dependence on single foreign markets — together broadening EU trade defense beyond narrow anti-dumping cases to the wider impact of China's export-led model.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: These three instruments are the concrete legislative vehicles to track; passage of the Cybersecurity Act revision in particular would create direct market-access risk for Chinese tech vendors in the EU.

insights.claim: China's Ministry of Commerce has sent written objections to the European Commission and member states calling the Industrial Accelerator Act 'a serious investment barrier,' is lobbying European business groups, and is planning an EU visit by Commerce Minister Wang Wentao — evidence that Beijing takes the EU's new toolbox seriously.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: Beijing's active lobbying against the IAA is itself a signal of the instrument's expected effectiveness — a useful sentiment indicator for how much bite the EU toolbox could have if enacted.
insights.locator: The Chinese Ministry of Commerce has sent written objections

insights.claim: The author's core thesis is that the EU should confront China's trade imbalances now rather than wait for the October European Council or the 2027 French election, because delay only strengthens China's economic defenses while narrowing the EU's options and raising the eventual cost of any confrontation.
insights.speaker: Grzegorz Stec
insights.so_what: Sets the base-case timeline for EU-China trade friction: expect the real test of EU resolve at the October European Council, with the outcome likely to shape trade-policy risk for China-exposed EU industrials through 2027.
insights.quote: The longer the EU waits, the narrower its options — and the higher the eventual price.
